title: Month-End Close & WIP
subhead: The most important concept in engineering finance: Work-In-Progress (WIP).

chunks.title: Why We Close the Books
chunks.bodyHtml:
At the end of every month, we finalize all transactions so Skarion leadership knows our exact profit margin per fiber mile.

For an engineering firm, the hardest part of month-end isn't paying bills—it's calculating exactly how much revenue we 'earned' even if we haven't billed the client yet.

steps.title: Day 1: Timesheets
steps.desc: Lock all US engineer timesheets and offshore contractor invoices.

steps.title: Day 2: Accrue Subcontractors
steps.desc: Post journal entries for offshore design work that is finished but unbilled.

steps.title: Day 3: Calculate WIP
steps.desc: Work-In-Progress: Calculate the value of partially completed fiber designs.

steps.title: Day 4: Revenue Recognition
steps.desc: Recognize revenue based on the percentage-of-completion method.

steps.title: Day 5: Project Financials
steps.desc: Generate Profit & Loss statements for each specific fiber buildout.

chunks.intro: WIP and Accruals are tricky. Let's see how you do.
chunks.title: Month-End Knowledge Check

questions.q: Skarion is halfway done with a 100-mile fiber design for a client. We have spent $10,000 on labor, but we can't bill the client until the 100 miles are fully delivered. How do we account for the $10,000 at month-end?
options: Record it as a total loss
Wait until next year to record it
questions.explanation: Because the work will eventually turn into revenue, we 'park' those costs on the Balance Sheet as a WIP Asset until the project is delivered and billed.
